name: MCPHallu
description: MCPHallu is a benchmark for diagnosing hallucinations in MCP (Model Context Protocol) agents along three axes: reasoning, execution, and memory. It contains 358 tasks across five capability domains (General, Analytics, Productivity, Financial, Coding) and four hallucination subtypes (Branch Collapse, Unreachable Goal, Tool Misuse, Context Forgetting), evaluated against 36 production MCP servers running in isolated Docker containers.
rai:dataCollection: Tasks were constructed in four stages: (1) tool grounding — tool names, parameter schemas, and return-value specifications were extracted from 36 live MCP servers via the MCP list-tools endpoint; (2) adversarial synthesis — Claude Sonnet 4.6 was prompted with subtype-specific templates to generate candidate tasks targeting one of four hallucination subtypes; (3) automated filtering and ground-truth execution — candidates were screened on five criteria (executability, hallucination coverage, self-containedness, non-triviality, label consistency via a separate GPT-4o solver run 5 times per task), and dynamic reference scripts were executed against live servers to produce verified ground-truth logs with write-operation rollback; (4) expert review by three annotators with 2+ years of LLM agent evaluation experience, each independently rating task realism, label correctness, and ground-truth validity on a 3-point scale (accepted if ≥2 on all criteria from ≥2 of 3 annotators).
rai:dataUseCases: Benchmarking and diagnosing hallucination failure modes in LLM-based MCP agents; subtype-level attribution of reasoning, execution, and memory failures.
rai:dataBiases: The 36 MCP servers are English-language and primarily target Western SaaS APIs (GitHub, Notion, Slack, etc.). Findings may not generalize to regional services or non-English-language tools.
rai:personalSensitiveInformation: No personal or sensitive information. All tasks are synthetic and constructed from public MCP server tool schemas.
rai:dataLimitations: MCPHallu covers 36 English-language MCP servers from five capability domains; generalisation to non-English, regional, or enterprise-internal tool sets is not validated. The benchmark targets agentic hallucination specifically and is not designed for assessing raw language understanding, knowledge retrieval, or single-turn QA. Model scores reflect behaviour in an isolated Docker environment and may differ under production deployments with real user data, rate-limited APIs, or multi-user concurrency. This dataset is not recommended as a sole safety evaluation for deployment decisions.
rai:dataSocialImpact: Positive impacts: MCPHallu enables systematic identification of failure modes in MCP agents, helping developers detect and mitigate hallucinations before deployment, which can improve reliability and user trust in AI-assisted workflows. Negative impacts / risks: Publishing detailed adversarial task patterns could be misused to craft inputs that intentionally trigger agent failures. To mitigate this risk, task prompts are framed as diagnostic stimuli rather than jailbreak instructions, and the repository is released under a permissive open-source licence that requires attribution. No specific demographic group is disproportionately harmed by dataset misuse.

provenanceActivities.name: Tool Schema Collection
provenanceActivities.description: Tool names, parameter definitions, and descriptions were collected programmatically from 36 live MCP servers using the MCP list-tools API endpoint. Servers were selected to cover five capability domains: General, Analytics, Productivity, Financial, and Coding.

provenanceActivities.name: Adversarial Task Synthesis
provenanceActivities.description: Tasks were synthesised by prompting Claude Sonnet 4.6 with subtype-specific adversarial prompt templates. Each subtype (Branch Collapse, Unreachable Goal, Tool Misuse, Context Forgetting) had a dedicated prompt that instilled the corresponding hallucination-inducing structure. Raw LLM outputs were parsed into structured JSON.

provenanceActivities.name: Automated Filtering
provenanceActivities.description: Generated tasks were automatically filtered on five criteria: (1) Executability — every tool listed in required_tools exists in the catalog and accepts the referenced parameter schema; (2) Hallucination coverage — the task contains at least one structural feature that triggers the target subtype (e.g., ≥2 mutually exclusive branches for Branch Collapse), verified by parsing the claims field; (3) Self-containedness — the task instruction does not reference external assumptions beyond the provided tool set; (4) Non-triviality — the correct answer cannot be reached by enumerating tool outputs without honouring the dependency graph in claims; (5) Label consistency — a separate GPT-4o instance ran each candidate task 5 times and the dominant failure mode was required to match hallucination_type. Tasks failing any criterion were regenerated with an augmented prompt naming the violated criterion. Additionally, dynamic reference scripts for stateless tasks were executed against live MCP servers to verify ground-truth correctness, with write operations rolled back via compensating transactions.

provenanceActivities.name: Expert Review
provenanceActivities.description: Three annotators with 2+ years of LLM agent evaluation experience reviewed each task for correctness of ground truth, clarity of instruction, and alignment with the target hallucination subtype. Disagreements were resolved by majority vote.

rai:annotatorDemographics: Three annotators with backgrounds in LLM agent evaluation (each with 2+ years experience).

distribution.name: mcphallu_tasks.json
distribution.description: All 358 MCPHallu tasks in a single JSON array.

distribution.name: confusion_tasks.jsonl
distribution.description: 84 Tool Misuse tasks in JSONL format.

distribution.name: memory_tasks.jsonl
distribution.description: 82 Context Forgetting tasks in JSONL format.

distribution.name: reasoning_tasks.jsonl
distribution.description: 89 Branch Collapse tasks in JSONL format.

distribution.name: void_tasks.jsonl
distribution.description: 103 Unreachable Goal tasks in JSONL format.

recordSet.name: tasks
recordSet.description: Each record is one MCPHallu evaluation task.

field.name: bucket
field.description: Capability domain: GENERAL, ANALYTICS, PRODUCTIVITY, FINANCIAL, or CODING.

field.name: hallucination_type
field.description: Target hallucination subtype stored as the internal label: 'Reasoning Trap' (paper name: Branch Collapse), 'Void Trap' (paper name: Unreachable Goal), 'Confusion Trap' (paper name: Tool Misuse), or 'Memory Trap' (paper name: Context Forgetting).

field.name: difficulty
field.description: Difficulty level: Easy, Medium, or Hard.

field.name: task
field.description: Natural-language instruction given to the MCP agent.

field.name: available_tools
field.description: List of MCP tool names available to the agent.

field.name: ground_truth
field.description: Ground truth specification: dynamic_script or state_assertions.

field.name: claims
field.description: Ordered claim graph encoding the dependency structure of the correct trajectory.

field.name: should_stop_early
field.description: Whether correct behavior is to stop early (Unreachable Goal tasks).

prov:wasGeneratedBy.prov:label: MCP Tool Schema Collection
prov:wasGeneratedBy.sc:description: Tool names, parameter definitions, and descriptions were collected programmatically from 36 live MCP servers across five capability domains (General, Analytics, Productivity, Financial, Coding) using the MCP list-tools API. No human annotators were involved in this stage.

prov:wasGeneratedBy.prov:label: Adversarial Task Synthesis
prov:wasGeneratedBy.sc:description: Benchmark tasks were synthetically generated by prompting Claude Sonnet (Anthropic, version 4.6) with four subtype-specific adversarial prompt templates targeting Branch Collapse, Unreachable Goal, Tool Misuse, and Context Forgetting. Raw LLM outputs were parsed into structured JSON task objects.

prov:wasAttributedTo.prov:label: Claude Sonnet 4.6
prov:wasAttributedTo.sc:description: Anthropic Claude Sonnet 4.6, used as the primary synthetic generator. Prompted with four subtype-specific adversarial templates to produce structured JSON task objects.

prov:wasGeneratedBy.prov:label: Automated Filtering
prov:wasGeneratedBy.sc:description: Generated tasks were automatically filtered on five criteria: (1) Executability — every tool listed in required_tools exists in the catalog and accepts the referenced parameter schema; (2) Hallucination coverage — the task contains at least one structural feature that triggers the target subtype, verified by parsing the claims field; (3) Self-containedness — the task instruction does not reference external assumptions beyond the provided tool set; (4) Non-triviality — the correct answer cannot be reached by enumerating tool outputs without honouring the dependency graph in claims; (5) Label consistency — a separate GPT-4o instance ran each candidate task 5 times and the dominant failure mode was required to match hallucination_type. Tasks failing any criterion were regenerated with an augmented prompt. Additionally, dynamic reference scripts for stateless tasks were executed against live MCP servers to verify ground-truth correctness, with write operations rolled back via compensating transactions.

prov:wasAttributedTo.prov:label: SoftwareAgent
prov:wasAttributedTo.sc:description: Custom Python scripts implementing five automated filtering criteria and ground-truth reference-script execution with write-operation rollback (generate_tasks_from_prompt.py and run_gt_execution.py).

prov:wasAttributedTo.prov:label: GPT-4o
prov:wasAttributedTo.sc:description: OpenAI GPT-4o used as an independent solver agent for the Label Consistency criterion (criterion 5). Each candidate task was run 5 times by GPT-4o; tasks whose dominant failure mode did not match the annotated hallucination_type were rejected and regenerated.

prov:wasGeneratedBy.prov:label: Expert Review
prov:wasGeneratedBy.sc:description: Three expert annotators with 2+ years of LLM agent evaluation experience performed quality review. Each task was independently reviewed for ground truth correctness, instruction clarity, and alignment with the target hallucination subtype. Disagreements were resolved by majority vote. Annotators were proficient in English and had backgrounds in NLP and AI agent research.

prov:wasAttributedTo.prov:label: Expert Annotators
prov:wasAttributedTo.sc:description: Three expert reviewers with 2+ years of LLM agent evaluation experience. English-proficient, backgrounds in NLP and AI agent research. Majority vote for disagreement resolution.
